create-focused-commits.label: Create Focused Commits
create-focused-commits.summary: Record one coherent staged change with a concise message, leave unrelated work outside it, and verify the result.

sketch0.title: A Commit Is a Deliberate Checkpoint

sketch1.title: Only the Staging Area Enters History

sketch2.title: Write for the Person Reading Later

sketch3.title: Verify the Checkpoint and the Remainder

try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.title: Commit One Coherent Change
try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The navigation files are staged. Commit them with `Add events navigation`, then inspect the latest one-line entry.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.hint: Record only the staged snapshot and use the message requested by the scenario.

help.concept: A focused commit records one understandable idea with a message that explains the result.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the project result, not the commands you used.
help.hint_2: After recording it, inspect history and any work that remains.

try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Add events navigation"
git log -1 --oneline
try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The navigation files are staged. Commit them with `Add events navigation`, then inspect the latest one-line entry.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html nav.js

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html nav.js

try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.title: Leave Unrelated Work for Later
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Only `README.md` is staged; `styles.css` is still an unstaged experiment. Commit the documentation change as `Clarify volunteer hours` and verify that the style edit remains.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.hint: Record only the staged snapshot and use the message requested by the scenario.

help.concept: Committing staged work does not sweep unrelated unstaged edits into history.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the project result, not the commands you used.
help.hint_2: After recording it, inspect history and any work that remains.

try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.starterCode: Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Clarify volunteer hours"
git status --short
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Only `README.md` is staged; `styles.css` is still an unstaged experiment. Commit the documentation change as `Clarify volunteer hours` and verify that the style edit remains.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'Open Saturdays.
' > README.md
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'body { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon.
' > README.md
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'body { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- README.md

printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon.
' > README.md

' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- README.md

try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.title: Use an Imperative Commit Message
try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

A bug fix in `app.js` is staged. Commit it with `Fix event card toggle`, then print only the latest subject line.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.hint: Record only the staged snapshot and use the message requested by the scenario.

help.concept: Imperative messages describe what the snapshot does when applied to the project.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the project result, not the commands you used.
help.hint_2: After recording it, inspect history and any work that remains.

try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Fix event card toggle"
git log -1 --format=%s
try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

A bug fix in `app.js` is staged. Commit it with `Fix event card toggle`, then print only the latest subject line.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = false;
' > app.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = !open;
' > app.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- app.js

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = false;
' > app.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = !open;
' > app.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- app.js

try_create_focused_commits_sketch3.title: Read the Story You Created
try_create_focused_commits_sketch3.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The site now has three focused snapshots. Read the one-line history and confirm that the working tree is clean.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch3.hint: Read the timeline from newest checkpoint to oldest.

help.concept: A sequence of focused commits reads like a concise explanation of how the project grew.
help.hint_1: Focus on commit order and subject lines rather than machine-specific hashes.
help.hint_2: The newest entry should describe the result from this milestone.

#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf community-site mkdir -p community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Start community site' cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

Events
' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add events navigation' cd community-site printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon. ' > README.md cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Clarify volunteer hours'

Events

single-choice-1.title: Commit History Inspection
single-choice-1.prompt: Which command provides a concise view of the commit history?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Record one coherent staged change with a concise message, leave unrelated work outside it, and verify the result.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

multi-choice-1.title: Purpose of Commits
multi-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of creating commits in Git?
multi-choice-1.hint: Choose the one answer that describes this command or concept precisely.

help.concept: Record one coherent staged change with a concise message, leave unrelated work outside it, and verify the result.
help.hint_1: Remove choices that act on a different repository layer or promise behavior Git does not provide.
help.hint_2: Prefer the exact definition used in this chapter over a nearby Git idea.

multi-choice-2.title: Commit Message Best Practices
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which of the following are best practices for writing commit messages?
multi-choice-2.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Record one coherent staged change with a concise message, leave unrelated work outside it, and verify the result.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Commit Process Steps
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to create a commit in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Record one coherent staged change with a concise message, leave unrelated work outside it, and verify the result.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

sketch0.title: A Commit Is a Deliberate Checkpoint
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A commit is more than a saved moment. It is a named checkpoint in the project’s explanation. It records the staged files, author, message, and relationship to the previous commit.

Before creating one, ask a simple question: **what single meaningful result becomes true here?** The answer should fit both the staged contents and the message.

If the answer contains two unrelated ideas joined by “and,” the snapshot may need a cleaner boundary.

After reviewing the staged snapshot, record one clear result and inspect it immediately:

```bash
git commit -m "Add workshop calendar"
git log -1 --oneline
```

The example message describes what became true. The one-entry log confirms that the intended subject reached history.

sketch1.title: Only the Staging Area Enters History
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A commit records the staging area—not every change in the folder. That allows `README.md` to enter history while an experimental `styles.css` edit remains in the working tree.

This behavior is a feature, not a missing step. It lets you record finished work without hiding or discarding unfinished work.

After committing, status should prove that the unrelated edit is still present and the committed path is no longer staged.

A commit can be complete even while unrelated work remains:

```bash
git commit -m "Clarify registration hours"
git status --short
```

Only the staged documentation enters history. Status should still show any separate experiment that was deliberately left unstaged.

sketch2.title: Write for the Person Reading Later
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A good subject is brief, specific, and written as an action: `Fix event card toggle`, not `changes` or `worked on app`.

Imagine the sentence “This commit will …” before the message. The completed sentence should describe the project result, not the commands used to produce it.

Readable messages turn the one-line log into a useful project summary.

When the exact subject matters, ask Git to print only that field:

```bash
git commit -m "Fix signup button"
git log -1 --format=%s
```

`%s` means the commit subject. This check avoids relying on a changing commit identifier.

sketch3.title: Verify the Checkpoint and the Remainder
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
After committing, inspect the newest subject and then inspect status. The first view proves what entered history. The second proves what still remains outside it.

A clean result is appropriate only when the task intended to finish all work. In a focused-commit exercise, a remaining unstaged edit may be exactly the evidence you need.

Verification should match the scenario, not a blanket desire for empty output.

Finish by reading the story and the remaining work:

```bash
git log --oneline
git status --short
```

The history should contain focused subjects in newest-first order. Status explains whether the repository is finished or whether another change still waits outside history.
